evidence.title: Management of Sore Throat: Time to Update | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Because rheumatic heart disease is so rare in the United States, the primary benefit of using antibiotics is symptom reduction, with a number needed to treat of 6 for improvement by day 3, and possibly the reduction of spread to others (although most close contacts have likely already been exposed by the time the patient is evaluated). 15 , 16 In 2023, an international group of experts in primary care respiratory infections published the first international guideline for the management of sore throat . 3 The guideline focuses on avoiding testing and antibiotic use for patients with uncomplicated sore throat (i.e., mild to moderate sore throat accompanied by cough with no red flags), reserving evaluation, testing, and possibly antibiotic use for those at risk of complicated or severe disease. 3 Using rapid antigen testing for streptococcus, followed by prescribing antibiotics, is not reco

evidence.title: Pharyngitis - sore throat: MedlinePlus Medical Encyclopedia
evidence.snippet: Drink soothing liquids. You can either drink warm liquids, such as lemon tea with honey, or cold liquids, such as ice water. You could also suck on a fruit-flavored ice pop. Gargle several times a day with warm salt water (1/2 teaspoon or 3 grams of salt in 1 cup or 240 milliliters of water). Suck on hard candies or throat lozenges. Young children should not be given these products because they can choke on them. Use of a cool-mist vaporizer or humidifier can moisten the air and soothe a dry and painful throat. Try over-the-counter pain medicines, such as acetaminophen (Tylenol) or ibuprofen (Advil, Motrin). Possible Complications

evidence.title: Strep A Test: MedlinePlus Medical Test
evidence.snippet: If you or your child is diagnosed with strep throat, you will need to take antibiotics. Most often you will need to take them for 10 days. After a day or two of taking the medicine, you or your child should start to feel better. Most people are no longer contagious after taking antibiotics for 24 hours. But it's important to take all the medicine as prescribed. Stopping early can lead to serious complications, such as:

note_text:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with a sore throat that started 2 days ago. She reports associated fever and chills, pain with swallowing but no difficulty breathing or swallowing. She notes swollen glands in her neck and visible white patches or pus on her tonsils. She denies cough, runny nose, nasal congestion, rash, joint pain, or swelling. She has not tried any treatments yet. Her son had strep throat last week, and she has no history of rheumatic fever or heart valve problems. No allergies or relevant comorbidities are reported.

O: Phone-only encounter; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is acute streptococcal pharyngitis given the sore throat with fever, tonsillar exudate, tender cervical lymphadenopathy, and recent exposure to a confirmed case in her son.
Differential:
1. Acute streptococcal pharyngitis
2. Viral pharyngitis
3. Infectious mononucleosis
4. Peritonsillar abscess (less likely without trismus or severe dysphagia)

P: - First-line medication: Penicillin V 500 mg orally twice daily for 10 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Alternatives: Amoxicillin 500 mg orally twice daily for 10 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Medication safety considerations: No allergies, no pregnancy, no renal or liver disease reported; monitor for allergic reactions
- Follow-up recommendations: Seek in-person medical care urgently if difficulty breathing, severe swallowing difficulty, or worsening symptoms occur; seek emergency care if airway obstruction develops
- Testing: Arrange for rapid antigen detection test or throat culture to confirm streptococcal infection if possible
